concept_ref: §3 row 3 — 'Decision coverage = Branch Condition coverage' (corrected by §5.3.4)

scenario.title: Two-Factor Login
scenario.narrative: A login function admits a user only when they are logged in AND have permission. Pick the test set that satisfies BOTH 100% Decision coverage (overall outcome flips T and F) AND 100% Branch Condition coverage (each individual condition observed T and F).

claim: Which candidate test set satisfies BOTH Decision coverage AND Branch Condition coverage for `is_logged_in AND has_permission`?

options.label: Set A — { (T,T) → show, (F,F) → error }
options.explanation: TRAP. Decision coverage IS 100% (one T outcome, one F outcome). But A and B always move together — A never appears T while B is F (and vice versa), so Branch Condition is NOT satisfied. A real bug where the permission check is bypassed when the user is logged in stays hidden.

options.label: Set B — { (T,T) → show, (T,F) → error, (F,T) → error }
options.explanation: Correct. (T,T) gives the TRUE outcome, (T,F) and (F,T) give FALSE outcomes — Decision is covered. A is observed T (in T,T and T,F) and F (in F,T); B is observed T (in T,T and F,T) and F (in T,F). Both Decision AND Branch Condition coverage are satisfied.

options.label: Set C — { (T,T) → show }
options.explanation: Only the TRUE outcome of the decision is exercised. The FALSE branch (error path) is never reached, so Decision coverage is 50%, not 100%.

options.label: Set D — { (T,T) → show, (F,T) → error }
options.explanation: Decision is satisfied (T then F outcome), and A flips T→F. But B is stuck at TRUE in both tests — Branch Condition for B is NOT satisfied (no F observation).

correct_answer_explanation: Decision coverage only asks whether the overall if-statement produced both T and F outcomes. Branch Condition is stricter: each condition must independently appear as T and F across the test set.
wrong_answer_explanation: If A and B always move together, you can flip the decision without flipping each condition. That's Decision-without-BC — Concept Analysis §3 row 3.
hints: Decision testing only asks: did the if-statement produce both TRUE and FALSE outcomes overall? Branch Condition asks something stricter — re-read Law Card 4.
Look at A and B separately. Did A appear as both TRUE and FALSE across the tests? What about B? Are any of them stuck on a single value?

Solution — Set B uses three diagonal tests. (T,T) gives T outcome; (T,F) and (F,T) give F outcomes (Decision ✓); A and B each appear T and F (BC ✓). Set A is the classic 'diagonal trap' — Decision passes but BC fails.

seeded_faults.description: Permission check is silently skipped — a user with is_logged_in=True is granted access regardless of has_permission.

misconceptions.explanation_md: Decision coverage tests only the overall outcome of the predicate (T/F at the if-statement). Branch Condition coverage requires *each individual condition* to take both TRUE and FALSE across the test set. With T1=(T,T) and T2=(F,F), A and B move together — a real-world bug where the permission check is bypassed when the user is logged in stays hidden.
